pair_convention.spot_explanation: AUD/JPY at 112.80 means 1 AUD buys 112.80 JPY. A rising pair indicates AUD strengthening versus JPY — the Australian Dollar appreciates relative to the Japanese Yen. A falling pair indicates AUD weakening versus JPY — the Japanese Yen appreciates relative to the Australian Dollar. At current levels the pair sits approximately +23.8% above OECD PPP fair value of ¥90.1.
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (AUD weakens vs JPY — JPY appreciates)

executive_summary: AUD/JPY trades at ¥112.80 as of 11 March 2026, having surged from the October 2025 trough near ¥86 — a rally of approximately 31% — driven by a remarkable bilateral narrative reversal: the RBA became the first G10 central bank to hike from a cutting cycle (3.85%), while the BoJ cautiously normalises from its negative-rate era at just 0.75%. The pair has appreciated roughly +8% year-to-date and reached a fresh 36-year high on 25 February 2026 after Australia announced stronger-than-expected headline inflation, demonstrating the power of the RBA-BoJ policy divergence story. Yet the pair now sits at a critical juncture: the Iran conflict's asymmetric impact — oil above $87/bbl is mildly supportive for AUD but substantially negative for JPY via energy import costs — has temporarily widened the pair beyond what bilateral rate differentials alone justify. The dominant bilateral narrative is a tug-of-war between AUD's exceptional carry advantage (+310bps annualised) and JPY's growing safe-haven premium plus incipient BoJ normalisation trajectory. AUD/JPY is highly sensitive to three factors — the interest-rate differential, global risk appetite, and inflation trends in both countries — and the pair trends when these factors align but becomes volatile when they diverge. Our headline tactical stance is Neutral (Conviction 2/5): the carry story is powerful but AUD positioning at the 100th CFTC percentile, elevated VIX, and approaching BoJ catalysts (Shunto, April hike) create near-term two-way risk. Strategically we are Mildly Bearish (Conviction 3/5) on a 1–6 month view as BoJ rate convergence, the AUD PPP overvaluation versus JPY of +23.8%, fiscal year-end repatriation, and the structural carry unwind argument combine to favour a gradual pair decline toward 105–108.

market_context.valuation_assessment: AUD/JPY is the most structurally overvalued bilateral in the dossier universe. AUD's OECD PPP fair value versus JPY is approximately ¥90.1, against a current spot of ¥112.80 — implying +23.8% above PPP fair value. JPY's own BIS REER stands at the all-time low of 67.73 (0th percentile), while AUD's REER at 110.52 is at the 63rd percentile. The compound mispricing signals are the strongest in G10: both the absolute premium over PPP and the REER disparity make this the pair most exposed to mean-reversion catalysts. The market's justification for the premium is the +310bps rate differential — but even on a discounted carry model, sustaining ¥112+ requires the differential to remain near current levels for 2+ years with no vol spike. That assumption is increasingly fragile as BoJ hikes accumulate.

positioning.net_pair_implication: The double-long AUD / double-short JPY configuration means net speculative positioning in AUD/JPY terms is extraordinarily extended — these are mirror-image carry trades that double the unwind risk. When carry unwinds, longs in the high-yield leg (AUD) and shorts in the funding leg (JPY) liquidate simultaneously, creating a multiplied and non-linear reversal.

market_context.recent_regime: In contrast to JPY headwinds, the RBA is hiking into an upside inflation surprise and is now among the more hawkish G10 central banks. AUD/JPY is up around 4–5% year-to-date, with the cross trading near the upper end of its 2026 range as AUD has rallied and JPY has weakened materially. With wide positive carry in favour of AUD and the yen likely to stay soft until after Japan's lower-house election and clearer BoJ action, the backdrop remains supportive. However, the Iran conflict since 28 February has introduced a new dynamic: Japan's extreme energy import vulnerability creates a competing bearish JPY narrative that temporarily reinforces the pair's upside, but the safe-haven bid for JPY creates episodic sharp pullbacks when risk deteriorates.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately ¥0.27 (27 pips) against the AUD/JPY long before carry is entirely erased over 4 weeks — a narrow buffer given daily ranges of 80–130 pips.
bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The AUD/JPY carry differential is the defining feature of this pair and represents the single most important bilateral factor. The RBA at 3.85% versus the BoJ at 0.75% generates an annualised carry of +310bps in favour of long AUD/JPY. At current pair-level IV of approximately 12.5%, the carry-to-vol ratio calculates to 0.248 — meaningful positive carry per unit of volatility, but below the ~0.4 threshold that signals carry is highly attractive relative to risk. Daily carry accrual at +310bps annualised: 310bps ÷ 252 = +1.23bps per trading day = approximately ¥0.14 per day at ¥112.80. Over the 20-trading-day tactical window, cumulative carry is approximately +24.6bps ≈ +¥27 pips. The carry regime for AUD/JPY has been constructive YTD, with the pair rallying ~+8% while delivering positive carry daily — generating strong total returns. However, the regime is under threat: VIX at 22.8 (with spikes to 35+) represents the volatility level at which carry strategies historically face negative Sharpe ratios. Carry trades in AUD/JPY work best when both the high-yield and funding legs are stable; currently, both are sensitive to data and central bank communication, reducing overall stability. Our base case expects AUD/JPY at 105–108 in 6 months — approximately 450–780 pips below the 6M forward outright, representing the quantifiable opportunity in the bear case.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: -0.02 from Policy & Rates, redirected to Factor 3 (Risk Appetite +0.02). tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral rate differential is the largest in G10 at +310bps (RBA 3.85% vs BoJ 0.75%), but the tactical signal is neutral because neither central bank is expected to move at their immediate meetings. The RBA holds 17 March (38% hike probability) while the BoJ holds 18 March (95% probability). A simultaneous hold-hold outcome — the most likely tactical event — generates no differential change and likely triggers a modest AUD/JPY pullback as the 'live meeting' event premium drains. The 10-year yield spread stands at approximately +266bps (ACGB 4.97% vs JGB 2.18%), itself the widest AU-JP yield spread since the early 1990s. However, the trajectory of this spread is now working against the pair: the BoJ dossier confirms JGB 10Y has risen ~80bps over 6 months, narrowing the UST-JGB spread from ~250bps to 199bps. The AU-JP 10Y spread will be similarly compressed by BoJ normalisation. Tactically, the rate story is fully priced — the market knows the RBA is hawkish and the BoJ is normalising — and the marginal surprise risk is a BoJ hawkish signal (Shunto) that compresses the differential faster than expected.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the bilateral rate differential is unambiguously on a convergence path, favouring AUD/JPY decline. The BoJ's path to 1.00%–1.25% by year-end (adding +25–50bps) combined with the RBA potentially pausing after reaching 4.10%–4.20% creates a narrowing dynamic. Each 50bps of AU-JP rate differential compression has historically correlated with approximately ¥7–10 of AUD/JPY decline based on the bilateral relationship observed during the 2012–2015 and 2022–2023 normalisation periods. If the differential narrows from +310bps to +235bps over 6 months (BoJ to 1.00%, RBA at 4.10%), that implies an approximately 7–14 pip decline in AUD/JPY from differential compression alone — without any risk-off amplification. The 10-year spread compression would add to this: if AU-JP 10Y narrows from +279bps to +225bps (BoJ raising short end, JGB 10Y rising toward 2.50%), the fundamental valuation model shifts to support ¥105–¥108. The RBA's own terminal rate at ~4.20–4.35% provides a ceiling to AU-JP spread widening, while the BoJ's neutral rate at 1.0–2.5% creates a structural floor for Japanese yields.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The inflation and growth differential unambiguously favours AUD/JPY upside on a standalone basis. Australia's trimmed mean CPI at 3.4% YoY vastly exceeds Japan's core CPI at 2.0% (ex-fresh food), and the growth differential of +2.5pp (AUD GDP 2.6% vs JPY GDP 1.1%) is among the largest in the G10/JPY complex. Tactically, the upcoming releases — Australia's February monthly CPI (25 March) and Japan's March Shunto results (12 March) — create two-sided event risk. Australian data is expected to be hot (consensus 3.6–3.7% headline); if confirmed, this validates the RBA hiking case and supports AUD/JPY. Japan's Shunto is the critical wildcard: Rengo's demand of 5.85–5.94% suggests results will likely exceed 5.5%, which would be the first sign that the bilateral inflation dynamic is converging (Japan wages accelerating, Australia's wage growth at 3.4% WPI). Per-capita GDP has been positive in Australia for four consecutive quarters, while Japan's +1.3% annualised Q4 2025 print remains well below Australia's run-rate.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the inflation and growth dynamic is converging rather than diverging. Australia's SoMP forecasts GDP decelerating to 1.8% by December 2026, while Japan's GDP growth of +1.3% annualised is expected to hold above potential (~0.5%). The growth gap narrows from +150bps to potentially +80–100bps by year-end. More importantly, Japan's wage acceleration (Shunto 5.5%+) shifts the underlying inflation dynamic: if Japan embeds 3%+ base pay growth, the domestic demand-driven inflation the BoJ requires for sustained normalisation is arriving. This narrows the policy justification gap — both central banks dealing with above-target underlying inflation, but from very different starting levels. The net assessment is neutral: the differential remains positive for AUD/JPY but the trajectory is unfavourable and the rate of convergence is the strategic risk that is underpriced.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: AUD/JPY is one of the purest barometers of global risk sentiment — it rallies in risk-on conditions when investors favour yield and retreats during risk-off phases that drive safety flows into the Yen. The Iran conflict has delivered the most sustained risk-off event since COVID, with VIX spiking to 35+ intraday (now ~22.8) and the Nikkei falling −8.5% from its February all-time high. AUD is simultaneously hit from two sides: its high-beta risk character drives carry unwinds (AUD selling), while JPY receives safe-haven inflows AND Japan's fiscal year-end repatriation is approaching. The net effect is AUD/JPY faces pressure from both legs — AUD longs liquidating and JPY shorts being bought. The pair's beta to the VIX on the downside is approximately −2 to −2.5x (i.e., a 10% VIX rise historically produces ~4–5% AUD/JPY decline) — this non-linear sensitivity is elevated in the current environment given the extreme positioning. Any further Iran escalation or equity market decline reprices the pair lower, potentially rapidly.

Conversely, a ceasefire that takes VIX to 16–18 would be pair-bullish, targeting ¥114–116.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the risk appetite factor normalises. Historical geopolitical shocks (Russia-Ukraine 2022, Gulf crises) produce risk-off effects with 2–3 month half-lives, after which carry resumes. If the Iran conflict resolves or contains within the strategic window, the risk-off premium dissipates and AUD/JPY resets toward carry-driven levels. However, the superannuation rebalancing dynamic (Australia's $4.3T AUM) provides mechanical support through equity recovery hedging. The strategic uncertainty is whether the conflict creates sustained above-$90 oil — if so, Japan's terms-of-trade deterioration is persistent, adding a structural headwind to JPY that caps pair downside. The bilateral risk assessment is genuinely neutral at the strategic horizon.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral positioning configuration is the most bearish-for-the-pair element of the entire framework. CFTC leveraged funds are simultaneously long AUD (+54,635 contracts, 100th percentile 3-year range) and short JPY (−34,225 contracts, net speculative −16,600 contracts). While neither leg alone is at an extreme contrarian threshold on absolute numbers, the bilateral combination for AUD/JPY represents a profoundly one-sided positioning — leveraged funds are maximally long in the carry trade direction. Open interest in AUD futures declining (283K to 267K) signals short-covering dominance rather than fresh buying, which is less durable. AUD/JPY is entering a critical zone where institutional flows suggest a potential medium-term reversal; the latest COT report shows AUD still net long but losing momentum, while JPY is seeing aggressive long accumulation — often an early signal of currency strengthening. Japan's fiscal year-end (31 March) creates well-documented seasonal JPY repatriation flows, adding a calendar-based tactical headwind. Carry trades usually build gradually but unwind quickly, so sudden declines can exceed previous gains.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the bilateral carry unwind is the dominant flow theme. Japan's life insurers at their lowest hedge ratio in 14 years hold an estimated USD-equivalent $150–200bn of unhedged foreign assets. As JPY strengthens and JGB yields rise toward 2.5%+, the incentive to hedge returns sharply — these forced hedge adjustments create structural JPY demand that does not appear in CFTC data. Japan's total stock of investment in the Australian economy stood at $282.9 billion in 2024, with $159.5 billion in direct investment — this bilateral investment relationship means Japanese institutional holders of Australian assets face currency losses as AUD/JPY declines, potentially triggering accelerated repatriation. Globally, JPY-funded carry trades are estimated at $1–2T notional — the unwinding is historically non-linear.

strategic.contrarian_case: The contrarian case for sustained AUD/JPY strength requires the carry regime to persist indefinitely — which requires: BoJ delays all hikes beyond June, VIX falls below 15 sustainably, and China stimulus lifts iron ore back above $110. This is possible (25% probability) but is the upside tail, not the base case. The strategic contrarian bear thesis is supported by Japan's $282.9bn bilateral investment stock in Australia at only 44.4–45.7% hedged — a 5% increase in hedge ratios generates ~$7.7bn of AUD/JPY selling equivalent to ~60,000 CFTC contracts, a massive structural flow not in any consensus model.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The net commodity exposure for AUD/JPY is genuinely complex and approximately offsetting tactically. Using the commodity direction rules: Oil ↑ → AUD mild positive, JPY strong negative → Net AUD/JPY RISES (JPY suffers more). Iron ore ↑ → AUD strong positive, JPY neutral → Net AUD/JPY RISES. LNG (JKM) ↑ → AUD mildly positive (Australian LNG exporter), JPY strongly negative (major LNG importer) → Net AUD/JPY RISES. Coal ↑ → AUD strong positive (major coal exporter), JPY negative (coal importer) → Net AUD/JPY RISES. At face value, the current commodity environment (oil $87–100, iron ore $102–106, coal $133–137, LNG $15.71 JKM) is net positive for AUD/JPY through the terms-of-trade channel. However, this is more than offset by the second-order effects: sustained high oil prices are contractionary for the global economy (negative for risk appetite and hence AUD/JPY via Factor 3). Tactically, commodity prices are range-bound with no breakout signal, so the net ToT contribution is approximately flat.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the external balance comparison turns negative for AUD/JPY. Australia's current account deficit at −2.9% of GDP is deteriorating toward −3.5% on the SoMP's own trajectory, driven by primary income outflows and compressing goods surplus. In stark bilateral contrast, Japan holds the world's largest net international investment position at $3,663bn, generating $41.59T in primary income surplus in 2025 — a structural current account surplus near +4.4% of GDP. This is arguably the sharpest bilateral external balance contrast in G10: Australia is a structural capital importer (needs foreign capital to fund the deficit), while Japan is a structural capital exporter (earning returns on its massive overseas assets). As JGB yields rise toward 2.5%+, the incentive for Japanese capital to repatriate from Australian assets grows — a structural flow headwind for AUD/JPY. Iron ore's consensus decline to $90–95/t over the strategic horizon adds further pressure on Australia's terms of trade, narrowing the AUD-positive commodity benefit.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: Australia is a major exporter of iron ore, coal, LNG, and agricultural commodities. Rising commodity prices are generally AUD-positive. Iron ore and coal are the dominant drivers.
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: Japan is a major importer of energy commodities — LNG, oil, and coal — with no significant commodity exports. Rising energy prices are strongly JPY-negative via terms-of-trade deterioration and energy import bill expansion.
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Oil: Oil ↑ → AUD mild positive (+); JPY strong negative (−−) → Net: AUD/JPY RISES (JPY suffers more). Step 2 — Iron ore: Iron ore ↑ → AUD strong positive (++); JPY neutral → Net: AUD/JPY RISES. Step 3 — LNG: LNG (JKM) ↑ → AUD mildly positive (Australia is an LNG exporter); JPY strongly negative (Japan is the world's largest LNG importer) → Net: AUD/JPY RISES. Step 4 — Coal: Coal ↑ → AUD strong positive (major coal exporter); JPY negative (coal importer) → Net: AUD/JPY RISES. Step 5 — Netting: All four major commodities point to AUD/JPY upside on price increases. However, second-order effects: sustained high oil is contractionary for global growth, which is negative for risk appetite and hence negative for AUD/JPY via the risk channel (Factor 3). Net tactical result: approximately flat as ToT tailwinds are offset by risk channel headwinds.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10% → Japan's energy import bill rises ~¥2–3T annually (JPY-negative, strong); AUD mild positive via commodity-linked currency. Net AUD/JPY impact: +¥100–200 pips near-term via ToT channel, but partially offset by global growth fears (risk-off) depressing AUD as well.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% → Japan's energy import bill falls ~¥2–3T annually (JPY-positive, strong); AUD mild negative via commodity-linked currency and improved risk sentiment. Net AUD/JPY impact: -¥80–160 pips near-term via ToT channel, partly offset by improved risk appetite (risk-on) lifting AUD.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Iron ore is the dominant commodity driver for AUD, while LNG/oil is dominant for JPY. For the AUD/JPY cross specifically, the dominant bilateral commodity driver is LNG/energy — because Japan is the world's largest LNG importer and Australia is a major LNG exporter, the LNG price creates a direct bilateral flow relationship (Japanese yen payments for Australian LNG = structural AUD demand / JPY selling).

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral guidance divergence is stark and pair-positive tactically. The RBA's communication has been unambiguously hawkish: Governor Bullock's 'every meeting is live' declaration, unanimous 9-0 February hike, SoMP revisions implying terminal rate of ~4.45%, and February minutes stating financial conditions 'may no longer be restrictive.' By contrast, the BoJ is attempting to hike but is constrained by PM Takaichi's political pressure. Stronger-than-expected Australian inflation data bolstered March RBA tightening expectations, allowing AUD/JPY to bust through the 110.00 barrier. At the same time, political pressure from Japanese PM Takaichi for the BOJ to refrain from hiking rates weighed heavily on the yen, followed by speculations that the latest central bank appointees would likely lean dovish. The result is a bilateral credibility gap that the market is exploiting: the RBA is credibly hawkish (follow-through demonstrated), while the BoJ's guidance is credibly dovish-conditional (hawkish intent but political constraints). This asymmetry supports AUD/JPY.

Takaichi board nominees: 2 reflationists (Asada, Sato) — dovish board composition shift
Gap between RBA & BoJ credibility: widest in G10 paired comparison

The bilateral edge is that the RBA is underpriced by ~25bps while the BoJ may be overpriced if Takaichi's board changes slow the hiking pace.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the guidance credibility dynamic shifts. The RBA's credibility is high near-term but faces risk from a potentially rapid deceleration in trimmed mean CPI toward 3.0% by late 2026 (SoMP projection) — if disinflation is faster than forecast, the market will aggressively reprice terminal lower. The BoJ's credibility is encumbered by Takaichi but anchored by Governor Ueda's independence through 2028. The strategic question is which credibility erosion happens first: RBA's if inflation falls faster than expected, or BoJ's if Takaichi's nominees derail the hiking path. These risks approximately offset over the strategic horizon, producing a neutral bilateral guidance signal.

strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices sustained RBA hawkishness and gradual BoJ normalisation — the base case. Surprises in either direction (RBA pause or BoJ acceleration) represent unpriced tail risks.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical bullish (60%) because RBA's demonstrated follow-through dominates bilateral guidance near-term. Strategic neutral (50%) because forecast credibility becomes uncertain over the horizon for both banks.

The pair-bearish events: Shunto results above 5.5% (12 March) pulling forward BoJ hike and JPY-positive, Japan fiscal year-end repatriation (31 March) providing structural JPY demand, any Iran escalation via VIX spike. The super central bank week (17–19 March) concentrates event vol, and the consecutive-day RBA/BoJ/FOMC schedule creates compounding outcomes where direction depends on the sequencing of surprises. Net: neutral because offsetting bilateral event risks.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, geopolitical risk is a known-unknown for both bilateral legs. A ceasefire removes the risk-off JPY premium (pair-bullish) but leaves Japan's energy import cost headwind (pair-neutral). A prolonged conflict keeps VIX elevated (pair-bearish via risk-off), dampens global growth, and compresses iron ore demand (pair-bearish via ToT). AUKUS/defence ties between Australia and Japan represent a unique bilateral geopolitical positive — deepening security alignment that supports long-term Australian investment attractiveness to Japanese capital. The Japan-China diplomatic friction creates a strategic tension: if it worsens, Japan's export exposure to China is affected, but Australian commodity demand from China also weakens (bilateral net: negative for pair). Strategic event risk is genuinely uncertain.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral fiscal contrast is not material for tactical AUD/JPY movements. Australia holds AAA/Aaa ratings with gross debt at 34.2% of GDP — among the lowest in G10. Japan holds A+/A1 ratings with gross debt at ~235% of GDP — the highest in the developed world. This sovereign quality differential normally creates a structural advantage for AUD in capital allocation models (Australian bonds are AAA vs A-rated Japanese bonds), which is a mild pair-supportive factor. However, neither fiscal situation is changing in a way that would move AUD/JPY over the tactical horizon: no rating reviews, no fiscal crises, and no immediate market pricing of fiscal deterioration in either country.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, Japan's fiscal expansion under Takaichi creates a subtle but important bilateral dynamic: record spending creates political pressure on the BoJ to slow normalisation (supporting wider differentials and pair stability), but simultaneously raises Japan's long-term fiscal risk premium (super-long JGB yields at all-time highs, 40Y at 4%). If JGB yields rise further due to fiscal concerns, it actually strengthens JPY by attracting repatriation — a counter-intuitive but historically observed relationship. The net strategic fiscal signal is neutral: Australia's fiscal quality supports the pair via sovereign allocation, while Japan's expanding fiscal stance creates mixed signals.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: USD liquidity conditions affect AUD/JPY through two competing channels. The benign USD liquidity backdrop (SOFR 3.65%, AUD/USD xccy basis near flat at +5 to +15bps, Fed RMPs at $40bn/month) supports AUD through the vehicle currency channel — cheap USD funding keeps carry trades open. However, Japan's fiscal year-end creates acute JPY/USD funding demand that tightens the JPY/USD basis (estimated −25 to −50bps, seasonally widening), making it marginally more expensive to hold USD vs JPY — a mild JPY-positive. The DXY at ~99 on Iran safe-haven demand is an intermediate headwind for AUD (commodity-linked currency) but the direct AUD/USD xccy basis remains benign. For this cross pair, USD liquidity conditions are less important than for USD pairs — the bilateral AUD/JPY dynamic is driven more by relative real rates and risk appetite than by absolute USD conditions. Net: neutral, with the fiscal year-end basis dynamic providing a marginal JPY tailwind.

tactical.score_interpretation: The pair is in genuine no-man's-land tactically — bullish fundamental forces (Inflation/Growth +0.117, Guidance +0.042) are almost exactly offset by bearish flow/positioning forces (Flows -0.070, Risk -0.078, Market Structure -0.030). tactical.rationale: The tactical AUD/JPY setup is defined by the collision of two roughly equal and opposing forces. On the bullish side, Australia's fundamental data is unambiguously superior: trimmed mean CPI at 3.4% versus Japan's 2.0%, GDP growth 2.6% versus 1.1%, and the RBA's unequivocally hawkish communication generating carry inflows daily at +310bps. These factors contribute +0.159 to the weighted score and represent the cleanest carry trade in the G10/JPY complex. On the bearish side, three factors deliver a near-equal -0.178: CFTC AUD positioning at the 100th percentile creates an asymmetric unwind risk (incremental buyers absent, stops concentrated), the Iran conflict keeps VIX elevated at 22.8 with proven spike capability to 35+ (compressing carry-to-vol ratios), and the pair's market structure shows rejection from multi-year highs with momentum compression. The bilateral carry community is the most extended it has been in several years — the double-long-AUD/double-short-JPY CFTC configuration is a classic pre-unwind setup. The Shunto wage results (12 March) are the single most important near-term catalyst: a result above 5.5% would pull forward BoJ April hike probability above 80%, directly compressing the bilateral rate differential and triggering a position unwind. The FOMC dot plot (18 March) is secondary — a dovish shift would be pair-positive (USD weakness supporting AUD), while a hawkish hold keeps USD bid and pressure on risk assets. The path of least resistance is a modest pullback toward ¥109–110 as positioning normalises and the Shunto catalyst resolves, before the pair stabilises pending the April BoJ meeting. Conviction of 2/5 reflects the genuine tactical balance — this is not the right moment to initiate a directional trade; risk management (reducing existing long AUD/JPY exposure, tightening stops to ¥109.20) is the appropriate tactical action.

strategic.score_interpretation: A score of -0.284 maps to Conviction 3/5 for the strategic bear thesis. The dominant contributors are Policy & Rates (-0.143) — reflecting rate differential convergence as BoJ hikes and RBA pauses — and External Balance (-0.088) — reflecting Japan's structural +4.4% CA surplus versus Australia's -2.9% CA deficit. These two factors alone contribute -0.231 of the -0.284 total, confirming that the strategic bear case is built on durable structural foundations rather than transient factors.
strategic.rationale: The strategic AUD/JPY bear thesis rests on four mutually reinforcing pillars. First and most important, the bilateral rate differential is on a structural convergence path: the BoJ hiking to 1.00–1.25% (confirmed as the consensus path) while the RBA likely pauses after reaching 4.10%–4.20% compresses the differential from +310bps toward +235–260bps over the horizon. Each 50bps of convergence historically correlates with approximately ¥7–10 of AUD/JPY decline, implying a fundamental rate-driven bear target of ¥103–108. Second, the external balance bilateral contrast is among the starkest in G10: Australia's CA at -2.9% of GDP is deteriorating (tariffs, iron ore headwinds, primary income outflows), while Japan's +4.4% CA surplus is structural and growing — the bilateral investment position of $282.9bn Japanese investment in Australia creates a large pool of potential repatriation flows that activates as JGB yields become competitive. Third, the bilateral carry community in AUD/JPY — the double-long-AUD/double-short-JPY CFTC configuration — is a classic late-cycle positioning extreme that historically precedes non-linear reversals once a catalyst materialises. The July 2024 analogue, where AUD/JPY fell ~18 big figures in weeks, demonstrates the pair's capacity for rapid repricing. Fourth, structural PPP overvaluation at +23.8% above fair value (¥90.1) represents a long-run gravitational force — mean reversion may take years but the direction is unambiguous and the pace accelerates with each BoJ hike. Where the bear thesis has genuine risk: the RBA's own hiking path could extend beyond 4.20% if inflation proves stickier than forecast, delaying differential compression; a China stimulus programme could lift iron ore above $110/t, improving Australia's terms of trade; and an Iran ceasefire could collapse the VIX-driven safe-haven JPY bid, temporarily bouncing the pair. These risks are assigned a 25% collective probability (the upside scenario) — real but not the base case. Conviction at 3/5 reflects confidence in direction but uncertainty in timing: the convergence takes place over months, punctuated by pullbacks, and requires patience through near-term volatility.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: AUD/JPY ranks as the 2nd most attractive short among all JPY crosses over the strategic horizon (behind EUR/JPY where the ECB-BoJ divergence is also compressing). Among all AUD crosses, AUD/JPY is the most attractive short/underweight — the combination of +23.8% PPP overvaluation, carry community crowding, and BoJ normalisation makes it the highest-conviction mean-reversion candidate. Among pure risk-proxy pairs, AUD/JPY's bear case is cleaner than NZD/JPY (NZD is cheaper, less crowded) or GBP/JPY (GBP has its own inflation problems complicating the JPY thesis).

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 2.2:1 on base case (4.3–8.7% gain vs 2.0–5.5% loss)
risk_reward.expected_value: (50% × +¥7.30) + (25% × -¥4.20) + (25% × -¥13.80) = +3.65 - 1.05 - 3.45 = -¥0.85 net; adjusted for carry cost of short (-310bps = ~-¥1.60 over 6 months): adjusted EV ≈ -¥2.45 per unit. However, base case directional gain of ~¥7.30 net of ~¥1.60 carry cost = ~+¥5.70 net per unit, supporting the short thesis.
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: The short position has negative carry (cost -310bps annualised) but structurally favourable directional asymmetry given the PPP overvaluation and rate convergence. The position requires conviction that the directional move exceeds carry costs — the base case ¥7–10 decline generates ~+5.5% net return versus the ~-1.4% carry cost, leaving approximately +4.1% net.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Aggregated bank forecast consensus for AUD/JPY shows early 2026 at approximately ¥99.20, with 1-month and 3-month forecasts pointing to ¥99.21 and ¥99.91 respectively (appears stale, pre-RBA February hike). More recent statistical models project AUD/JPY fluctuating between 114.45 and 119.13 by December 2026, with an average near ¥116.79. The range of consensus is exceptionally wide — from ¥99 to ¥119 year-end.
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Neutral (Conviction 2/5) — no directional position recommended into the March event cluster
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Bearish (Conviction 3/5) — target ¥103–108 by mid-2026 on a short AUD/JPY position
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: approximately -700 to -1,200 pips below the bull consensus of ¥115–116 by mid-2026
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Three key analytical divergences: (1) We apply a bilateral carry unwind framework — the double-long-AUD/double-short-JPY positioning extreme is not captured in consensus bank models, and the July 2024 analogue demonstrates the pair's capacity for -18 big figure moves; (2) We weight the external balance contrast more heavily than consensus — Japan's $3,663bn NIIP vs Australia's structural CA deficit creates asymmetric repatriation risk that accrues non-linearly as JGB yields become competitive (life insurer hedge ratio at 14-year lows creates a $155bn latent AUD/JPY selling flow); (3) We apply a BoJ normalisation acceleration probability that exceeds consensus — Shunto 5.5%+ would pull the market to 1.25%+ pricing, materially faster than the 'measured pace' assumption embedded in most bank forecasts.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: AUD/JPY reaches ¥105–108 against consensus ¥115–116, generating approximately 700–1,200 pips or 6–10% alpha on a short position.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: AUD/JPY extends to ¥116–119, generating approximately 300–620 pips loss — manageable with proper stops at ¥113.50.

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical and strategic views are directionally consistent (both bearish-leaning) but differ in conviction: tactical at 2/5 (near-neutral), strategic at 3/5 (moderate bear). The divergence reflects timing rather than thesis: the tactical near-zero score (-0.019) reflects genuine two-way event risk in the 17–19 March window, where a RBA hike or FOMC dovish surprise would bounce the pair meaningfully before the strategic bear thesis reasserts.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: April 27 BoJ meeting — if the BoJ hikes to 1.00%, the strategic conviction rises to 4/5 and the tactical entry becomes confirmed.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Do not initiate new short AUD/JPY positions at ¥112.80 into the March event cluster. Wait for the event premium to resolve (post-19 March), use any bounce toward ¥111–112 to establish strategic short positions, with a stop above ¥113.50 and a target of ¥105–108 by August 2026.

forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: -¥3.67 to -¥6.67 below forward (forward ¥113.67 vs expected ¥107–110) → edge: -367 to -667 pips on short AUD/JPY vs forward
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: -¥6.55 to -¥11.55 below forward (forward ¥114.55 vs expected ¥103–108) → edge: -655 to -1,155 pips on short AUD/JPY vs forward

catalysts.rationale: Japan's annual wage negotiation results are the single most important near-term catalyst for AUD/JPY. A Shunto result above 5.5% (Rengo demanded 5.85–5.94%) would immediately reprice BoJ April hike probability above 80%, compress the bilateral rate differential narrative, and trigger JPY short covering while simultaneously pressuring AUD carry longs. The bilateral transmission is direct: higher Shunto → BoJ April hike priced in → bilateral differential compresses -25bps → AUD/JPY falls ¥150–250 pips within 24 hours.

Hold expected at 95% probability. However, any statement language hawkishly referencing Shunto results or removing dovish risk caveats would be mildly JPY-positive. The BoJ's communication about Iran conflict risks is the key variable — if Ueda's caution from 4 March is maintained, it delays the April hike narrative. If language shifts toward April readiness, AUD/JPY falls. The bilateral transmission works through market repricing of the BoJ's reaction function rather than any direct rate change.

Market prices 38% hike probability. An RBA hike to 4.10% would widen the bilateral differential by +25bps, add AUD carry attractiveness, and boost AUD/JPY by ¥200–350 pips. A hawkish hold ('May is live') maintains the status quo. A dovish hold would collapse AUD carry expectations and trigger positioning unwind, driving AUD/JPY toward ¥108–109 rapidly. The bilateral transmission is direct via rate differential — each RBA action immediately impacts the +310bps carry advantage that defines the pair.

The sequential BoJ/FOMC on consecutive days creates compounding event risk. A dovish FOMC dot plot (3+ cuts signalled) would weaken USD, supporting AUD/JPY through the AUD leg. A hawkish BoJ surprise (unlikely) would strengthen JPY, pushing pair lower. The most likely outcome (both hold, neutral language) leaves the pair directionless after the event premium drains. Bilateral impact depends on sequencing — if BoJ hawkish signal precedes FOMC dovish, the net pair impact is ambiguous with JPY strength and USD weakness potentially offsetting.

First full monthly CPI post-RBA hike. Consensus expects 3.6–3.7%; a print at or above 3.8% would validate the May hike narrative and support AUD. This is the data event most likely to drive a counter-trend rally in AUD/JPY if the pair has corrected into the event on Shunto/BoJ repricing. Bilateral transmission: hot AU CPI → RBA May hike expectation solidified → AUD positive → AUD/JPY rallies ¥100–200 pips. The specific trimmed mean component matters most for the bilateral differential narrative.

Japan's fiscal year-end on 31 March is the most well-documented seasonal FX pattern in G10, producing ¥300–500 pips of JPY strength as Japanese institutions repatriate foreign income and close FX books. For AUD/JPY specifically, the bilateral investment stock of $282.9bn (Japan's investment in Australia) generates meaningful AUD/JPY selling pressure as Japanese asset managers realise FY2025 profits. The seasonal strength is concentrated in the final 7–10 trading days of March — precisely the tactical window. This bilateral flow dynamic is structural and calendar-driven, not dependent on any news catalyst.

catalysts.event: Japan April Tankan Survey & China NBS PMIs
catalysts.rationale: The Tankan at a 4-year high (+15 DI for large manufacturers) is a key BoJ input confirming business conditions. A Tankan reading above +18 would strengthen the BoJ's April hike case (JPY-positive, pair falls). China NBS PMIs below 50 would signal commodity demand weakness, hurting AUD through the iron ore channel (pair falls via base leg). A Tankan beat combined with China PMI miss is doubly bearish for AUD/JPY — the bilateral transmission works via BoJ rate expectations (JPY leg) and commodity demand (AUD leg) simultaneously.

The single most important event for the strategic AUD/JPY thesis. A 25bp hike to 1.00% would be the highest BoJ rate since 1995 and would trigger significant repricing of the bilateral differential. Market currently prices ~60% probability for this hike. A hike would compress the AU-JP differential from +310bps to +285bps and signal the convergence path is intact, likely driving AUD/JPY to ¥106–109 within days. The April Outlook Report with updated forecasts is equally important — any upward revision to the BoJ's CPI path accelerates the carry unwind thesis. This is the inflection catalyst for the strategic bear thesis.

A full 25bp RBA hike is already priced for May. If delivered, the market may respond with 'buy the rumour, sell the fact' — especially if Shunto/BoJ have already compressed the bilateral narrative. The May SoMP's updated trimmed mean forecasts will clarify whether Australia's inflation trajectory warrants further tightening beyond 4.10%, setting the parameters of the differential outlook for H2 2026. The bilateral impact depends on whether the SoMP implies further hiking (pair-supportive, widening differential) or a pause signal (pair-bearish, confirming convergence).

catalysts.event: FOMC June Meeting (potential first Fed cut)
catalysts.rationale: The first Fed cut would compress AU-US spreads slightly (mildly negative for AUD) while weakening USD globally (mildly positive for AUD vs USD). For AUD/JPY specifically, the impact is indirect — the Fed cut matters more for AUD/USD than AUD/JPY. The BoJ's response to the Fed cut (does it embolden a July hike?) matters more for the pair. The bilateral transmission is indirect: Fed cut → USD weaker → AUD/USD higher → partial AUD/JPY support, but offset if BoJ interprets Fed easing as enabling faster normalisation.

This is the key inflection for the strategic AUD/JPY thesis. If AU trimmed mean CPI is tracking toward the RBA's 3.7% mid-2026 SoMP forecast, the RBA remains in play for further hikes (pair-supportive). If CPI decelerates toward 3.0–3.2% faster than expected, the RBA pause narrative strengthens (pair-bearish as differential compression accelerates). The bilateral outcome depends on whether both central banks are converging (both hiking = differential stable) or one pausing (RBA pauses + BoJ hikes = differential compresses). This data point is the critical test of the convergence thesis.

invalidators.condition: BoJ explicitly signals an extended pause in their April 27 meeting statement using language such as 'monitoring period before further adjustments'
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral to Bullish for the pair
invalidators.mechanism: The primary strategic bear thesis (differential convergence) collapses without BoJ rate hikes. The AU-JP rate differential remains at +310bps with no convergence catalyst, restoring the carry regime and removing the fundamental justification for AUD/JPY decline.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BoJ statement language at each meeting — specifically forward guidance clauses and risk assessment language

invalidators.condition: Australia's trimmed mean CPI accelerates above 3.8% for two consecutive monthly readings (next two: 25 March and 29 April)
invalidators.mechanism: An RBA hike beyond 4.10% toward 4.35% would widen the bilateral differential from +310bps to +360bps, reversing the convergence narrative and reigniting carry inflows. The strategic bear thesis requires differential compression; widening differential is the opposite scenario.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: ABS Monthly CPI trimmed mean component

invalidators.condition: Bilateral CFTC positioning unwinds to AUD net below +20,000 contracts (below 50th percentile) AND JPY net short moves above -60,000 contracts simultaneously within 4 weeks
invalidators.new_stance: Bearish with higher conviction (3/5 to 4/5) for the pair
invalidators.mechanism: Positioned-cleared markets are the entry condition for structural shorts. The current extreme positioning (100th percentile AUD) is a contrarian warning signal — once it clears, the structural bear thesis can be expressed with higher conviction as the unwind catalyst has fired.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: CFTC COT report — AUD and JPY non-commercial net positions simultaneously

invalidators.condition: Iron ore rises and sustains above $110/t for 3+ consecutive weeks on China stimulus with NBS PMI returning above 51.0
invalidators.new_stance: Neutral to Bullish for the pair
invalidators.mechanism: Australia's terms of trade improvement would partially offset the rate differential compression and create fresh AUD demand from bilateral Japanese commodity buyers. The External Balance factor (currently -0.088 contribution) would shift to neutral or positive, removing one of the two primary structural bear pillars.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SGX Fe62 iron ore futures front month; China NBS Manufacturing PMI

invalidators.condition: AUD/JPY breaks AND sustains above ¥116.00 for 3+ consecutive trading days
invalidators.mechanism: A sustained break above ¥116 signals that either the BoJ has been completely captured by Takaichi's dovish nominees or the RBA has hiked beyond 4.35%, restoring the wide differential narrative. The technical and fundamental bear thesis both collapse above ¥116.

invalidators.condition: Japan's current account surplus falls below ¥20 trillion annualised (approximately ¥1.65T monthly) on sustained oil above $100 for 8+ weeks
invalidators.mechanism: The primary support floor for JPY (structural CA surplus) weakens materially, partially offsetting the rate convergence thesis. The External Balance factor (currently -0.088 strategic contribution) would shift toward zero as Japan's creditor status advantage diminishes under sustained energy import pressure.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: MoF monthly current account data; Brent crude oil front month

signposts.confirms_if: Narrows below +240bps (BoJ hike + RBA plateau = convergence confirmed)
signposts.invalidates_if: Widens above +310bps (RBA hikes again AND JGB yields stall)
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is THE primary driver of AUD/JPY — more relevant than AU-US or US-JP spreads for this specific cross. The 10Y spread narrows with each BoJ hike and each RBA pause, directly compressing carry attractiveness. Historical relationship suggests approximately ¥0.15–0.20 of AUD/JPY movement per basis point of spread change.

signposts.confirms_if: Shunto results exceed 5.5% (accelerates BoJ April hike probability above 80%)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Uniquely important for AUD/JPY because Shunto results directly determine the BoJ's hike calendar — the single most important bilateral catalyst for differential compression. No other indicator has as direct a transmission to the pair's fundamental driver (AU-JP rate differential) as the annual wage settlement.
signposts.frequency: Annual concentrated response (12 March 2026); monthly earnings data thereafter

signposts.indicator: CFTC AUD Net Non-Commercial Positioning (3-year COT Index)
signposts.confirms_if: Resets below +30,000 contracts (below 70th percentile) — carry unwind beginning
signposts.invalidates_if: Exceeds +80,000 absolute contracts (extreme crowding exceeded, historically rare — structural regime signal)
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD CFTC positioning is uniquely important for AUD/JPY because AUD longs are simultaneously JPY-carry shorts; both legs must be watched together for the bilateral unwind signal. The 100th percentile reading is the key near-term risk — no incremental buyers exist, making any catalyst trigger an asymmetric downside move.

signposts.confirms_if: Sustains above 25 for 5+ days (risk-off regime — carry unwind accelerates)
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 17 sustainably (risk-on resurgence — carry resumes, pair bounces)
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD/JPY has the highest VIX beta among major G10 crosses — AUD is a high-beta risk proxy while JPY is the primary safe-haven asset. VIX moves above 25 historically produce 3–5% AUD/JPY declines within 2–3 weeks. The bilateral VIX beta of approximately -1.2 to -1.5 per 10pt VIX move means this indicator is uniquely informative for this specific cross.

signposts.indicator: Japan Life Insurer FX Hedge Ratio (bilateral flow indicator)
signposts.current_level: Japan total investment in Australia: $282.9 billion (2024); life insurer FX hedge ratio: 44.4–45.7% (14-year low)
signposts.confirms_if: Japanese institutional AUD hedge ratios rise above 55% from current lows (repatriation flows accelerating)
signposts.invalidates_if: Japanese hedge ratios fall further below 40% (deepening unhedged long AUD — structural AUD/JPY support)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The bilateral investment relationship creates structural AUD/JPY flow dynamics not visible in CFTC data. A $282.9bn stock with only 44.4–45.7% hedged means roughly $155bn of currency exposure to AUD/JPY. Even a 5% increase in hedge ratios generates ~$7.7bn of AUD/JPY selling — equivalent to ~60,000 CFTC contracts. This is the biggest bilateral flow dynamic not in any consensus model.
signposts.source: Deloitte Australia / ABS Foreign Investment Statistics — https://www.deloitte.com/au/en/services/international-specialist-services/perspectives/japan-au-economic-security-through-stronger-ties.html

assumptions: Spot rate reconciliation: The AUD dossier records AUD/JPY at 111.50, while external bilateral research sources confirm the cross trading near ¥112.80 by 11 March 2026, consistent with a spot that moved higher on the post-Iran-conflict carry dynamics. Analysis uses ¥112.80 as the operative valuation-date spot.
The 50-day SMA data from bilateral research (¥109.14) and 200-day SMA (¥102.75) is sourced from model estimates as of the valuation date.

Carry calculations use policy rate differentials as a proxy for actual overnight swap rates. The actual AUD/JPY carry may differ by ±10–20bps depending on the exact term of the swap and broker spreads. Forward points are derived from interest rate parity and are indicative.
Bilateral investment data from Deloitte/FIRB is the most recent available (2024 vintage) and may understate the current position by up to $20–30bn given appreciation in Australian asset values.

CFTC bilateral positioning (the 'double-long-AUD/double-short-JPY' framing) is analytically constructed — CFTC reports AUD and JPY futures independently. The bilateral net position is an approximation that assumes correlated positioning in both contracts.

PPP fair value of ¥90.1 uses OECD 2024 GDP-based PPP data applied to current spot. This is a long-run equilibrium concept with a typical reversion timeframe of 5–10 years, not a near-term price target.

Forecast sources: Bank/model year-end forecasts range extremely widely (¥99 to ¥119) and should be treated as directional guidance only. The most analytically rigorous estimates incorporate post-February-RBA-hike assumptions; earlier estimates (sub-¥105) are likely stale.

The 52-week high is cited as both ¥111.56 (52-week observed high) and ¥112.09 (3 March 2026 current year high) in different sources. The pair subsequently made a fresh 36-year high on 25 February 2026 that extended above both of these levels to approximately ¥112.80. The range data is treated as approximate.

The AUD CFTC net position is cited as both +54,635 (leveraged funds only) and +67,762 (all non-commercial) in different parts of the markdown. Both figures are retained as cited, with the broader non-commercial figure used for percentile calculations.
